macro_segments.title: Intent and the Definition of Murder: Intention toward a different victim

micro_segments.title: Introductory framework: The role of intent in defining the act of murder
Cases where intent is directed toward a non-capital act (animal, non-Jew, fetus)
Intent must match both the severity of the act and the identity of the victim
Dispute over intent for the specific person: Rabbi Shimon vs. the Sages

macro_segments.title: Intent and the Definition of Murder: Intention toward a different victim (continued)

micro_segments.title: The Gemara clarifies which cases Rabbi Shimon disputes

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's principle: Murder requires intent toward the specific victim

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's abstract statement: 'Even if trying to kill this person and killing another, exempt'
Positive intent required: Must explicitly state 'I am intending for this person'
The murder of Franklin Roosevelt's would-be assassin: A real-world parallel to the Talmudic case
The source for Rabbi Shimon's position: The verse on lying in wait (ve'arav lo)

macro_segments.title: The Sages' position: Intent to commit murder is sufficient, even if the victim differs

micro_segments.title: The Sages' alternative reading of ve'arav lo: Intent to do a capital offense
The case of throwing a stone into a mixed crowd: Application of the principle that a fixed presence (kavua) is treated as 50-50
The principle of kol kavua kemachtza al machtza: Any fixed element in a group creates a 50-50 assessment
Contrast with kol defarish merubbah farish: When an individual separates from the majority, probability applies
Practical application in contemporary halacha: Women and non-Jewish populations

macro_segments.title: The dispute applied to the case of killing a woman while aiming at a man

micro_segments.title: The biblical case of fighting men and hitting a pregnant woman: Exodus 21:22-23
How the Sages explain the case: If she dies, it is execution (nefesh tachat nefesh)
Rabbi Shimon's challenge: The verse cannot mean execution in this case
Resolving the verse: Nefesh tachat nefesh means monetary payment (mamon), not execution

macro_segments.title: The Tanna of the House of Chizkiyah: Murder always results in exemption from monetary payment

micro_segments.title: The parallel verses on striking an animal versus striking a human
The Tanna rejects both Rabbi Shimon and the Sages on the question of monetary payment
The principle of adam muad le'olam: A person is always liable for damages from animals, never for human death
Distinctions that don't matter in murder: Intentional vs. unintentional, correct vs. wrong victim
The distinction between overriding other damages (kim leh) and the payment for life itself

macro_segments.title: The case of a murderer mixed with other murderers: Can they all be executed or must they be imprisoned?

micro_segments.title: The mishna: A murderer mixed with other murderers—all are exempt from execution
The Sages hold all are exempt; Rabbi Yehudah says all are imprisoned until death
The case of different types of execution being mixed: Stoning vs. burning vs. sword vs. strangulation
The meaning of 'other people': One murderer without a final judgment mixed with murderers who have received final judgment
The principle of gmar din: Judgment cannot be passed without the person's presence and identification
Reish Lakish's answer: The case involves an ox, not a human, that hasn't been judged yet

topical_tags.term: The specific person requirement (ad sheyomar leploni)
Kavua (fixed presence) and mechtzah al mechtzah (50-50 assessment)
Nefesh tachat nefesh (life for life) vs. monetary payment
Adam muad le'olam (permanent liability for damages)
Types of execution: stoning (skila), burning (sreifa), sword (sayaf), strangulation (chenek)
Four types of capital punishment (arba misos beit din)
Trying to kill one person and killing another (nitkavein laharog et zeh v'harag et zeh)
Prison (kippa) for murderers without final judgment
